schools.summary: The call to pass over Jordan to possess greater nations: 'hear, O Israel: Thou art to pass over Jordan this day, to go in to possess nations greater and mightier than thyself, cities great and fenced up to heaven' — Moses calls Israel to cross Jordan and possess nations stronger than themselves, with great walled cities (the Hear, O Israel: Thou art to pass over Jordan this day, the call to cross over, the to go in to possess nations greater and mightier than thyself, the stronger foe to be dispossessed, the cities great and fenced up to heaven, the mighty fortified cities, the figure, the conquest of stronger nations and walled cities, beyond Israel's own power, the truth that the victory would be God's, not Israel's, the obstacles great but God greater, the figure of the obstacles that faith must face, the giants and the walls that unbelief feared but faith overcomes, the call to go forward against a mightier foe in the strength of God, Joshua 1:9, the conquest that would prove God's power, not the people's); the call to possess stronger nations (the conquest) — the charge (to cross over); 'cities great and fenced up to heaven' (the mighty fortified cities, the obstacles, the foe stronger, Joshua 1:9, the figure, the obstacles overcome by God's power, the providence, the charge, God going before to destroy them to follow, vv.2-3) — Henry, Gill.

themes: Hear, O Israel: Thou art to pass over Jordan this day, to go in to possess nations greater and mightier than thyself, cities great and fenced up to heaven
the call to pass over Jordan to possess greater nations
Israel to cross Jordan and possess nations stronger than themselves, the victory God's and not their own
the obstacles great but God greater (Joshua 1:9)

schools.summary: The dreaded giants, the children of Anak: 'a people great and tall, the children of the Anakims, whom thou knowest, and of whom thou hast heard say, Who can stand before the children of Anak!' — the foe included the giant Anakims, of whom the proverb said none could stand before them (the a people great and tall, the children of the Anakims, the giant foe, the whom thou knowest, and of whom thou hast heard say, the dread known from the spies, Numbers 13:33, the Who can stand before the children of Anak!, the proverb of their terror, the figure, the dreaded giants, the foe that unbelief had feared at Kadesh, now to be faced, the truth that the same giants the spies feared God would now subdue before Israel, the figure of the terror of the foe that faith overcomes by God's power, the giants no obstacle to God, the proverb of their invincibility answered by the God who goes before, the obstacle that magnified God's victory, the Anakims given into Israel's hand); the giant Anakims (the dreaded foe, Numbers 13:33) — the giants (the Anakims); 'Who can stand before the children of Anak!' (the proverb of their terror, the foe feared, Numbers 13:33, the figure, the giants overcome by God, the providence, the foe, God going before as a consuming fire to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: A people great and tall, the children of the Anakims, whom thou knowest, and of whom thou hast heard say, Who can stand before the children of Anak!
the dreaded giants, the children of Anak
the giant Anakims, the foe that unbelief had feared at Kadesh, now to be subdued by the God who goes before
the obstacle that magnified his victory (Numbers 13:33)

schools.summary: God going before as a consuming fire to destroy the foe: 'understand therefore this day, that the LORD thy God is he which goeth over before thee; as a consuming fire he shall destroy them, and he shall bring them down before thy face: so shalt thou drive them out, and destroy them quickly, as the LORD hath said unto thee' — God himself would go before Israel as a consuming fire to destroy the nations (the the LORD thy God is he which goeth over before thee, God leading the way, the as a consuming fire he shall destroy them, God the destroyer of the foe, the he shall bring them down before thy face: so shalt thou drive them out, the foe subdued by God's power, the figure, God going before as a consuming fire, the victory his work, Israel but the instrument, the truth that the conquest would be God's act, the consuming fire destroying the foe before them, the figure of the God who fights for his people and destroys their enemies, 'the LORD thy God is a consuming fire,' Deuteronomy 4:24, the assurance that God would subdue the giants and walls, the victory swift and sure, the ground of confidence, God himself going before); God going before as a consuming fire (the destroyer, Deuteronomy 4:24) — the assurance (God before them); 'so shalt thou drive them out, and destroy them quickly' (the foe subdued by God, the swift victory, the figure, God fighting for his people, the providence, the assurance, the warning against self-righteousness to follow, vv.4) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Understand therefore this day, that the LORD thy God is he which goeth over before thee; as a consuming fire he shall destroy them, and he shall bring them down before thy face: so shalt thou drive them out, and destroy them quickly, as the LORD hath said unto thee
God going before as a consuming fire to destroy the foe
God himself going before Israel as a consuming fire, the conquest his act and not theirs
the victory swift and sure (Deuteronomy 4:24)

schools.summary: The warning not to ascribe the conquest to their own righteousness: 'speak not thou in thine heart, after that the LORD thy God hath cast them out from before thee, saying, For my righteousness the LORD hath brought me in to possess this land: but for the wickedness of these nations the LORD doth drive them out from before thee' — the disclaimer of merit — 'speak not thou in thine heart... For my righteousness the LORD hath brought me in to possess this land: but for the wickedness of these nations the LORD doth drive them out': Israel warned not to think they received the land for their own righteousness, but the nations were cast out for their wickedness (the witnesses noting the significance — the inheritance given not for Israel's merit but of God's free grace, the land not earned by their righteousness, but given of grace and the promise to the fathers, the nations driven out for their own sin, the lesson — that the people of God owe their inheritance to grace, not works, the express denial of merit, 'not for thy righteousness,' lest pride creep in, the warning against the self-righteousness that would claim God's gifts as earned, the truth that salvation and inheritance are of grace, not of human righteousness, 'not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us,' Titus 3:5, 'by grace are ye saved through faith... not of works,' Ephesians 2:8-9, the figure — the doctrine of free grace, the inheritance a gift, the wickedness of the nations the ground of their judgment but not the people's merit the ground of their blessing, the truth that boasting is excluded, the gift owned as God's, the figure of the humbling of the heart, that the people not ascribe to themselves what is God's free gift, the figure of the inheritance by grace, not by the people's righteousness); the warning against ascribing the inheritance to their own righteousness, the merit disclaimed (the gift of grace, Titus 3:5) — the warning (not for thy righteousness); the nations cast out for their own wickedness, not Israel's merit the ground of the blessing (the dual truth, grace to Israel and judgment on the nations, Ephesians 2:8-9) — the figure (the inheritance of free grace, boasting excluded, the providence, the warning, the dual ground of grace and promise to follow, vv.5) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why was the land given to Israel, if not for their righteousness?

sides.position: Of God's free grace and the promise to the fathers, not for Israel's merit - the nations driven out for their own wickedness but the people blessed by grace alone, a figure of the doctrine of salvation by grace not works (Titus 3:5; Ephesians 2:8-9), boasting excluded and the gift owned as God's

themes: Speak not thou in thine heart, after that the LORD thy God hath cast them out from before thee, saying, For my righteousness the LORD hath brought me in to possess this land: but for the wickedness of these nations the LORD doth drive them out from before thee
the warning not to ascribe the conquest to their own righteousness (the crux of grace, not works, in the inheritance)
the inheritance given of God's free grace and the promise, not earned by Israel's merit, the nations driven out for their own sin
a figure of the doctrine of salvation by grace not works, boasting excluded (Titus 3:5; Ephesians 2:8-9)

schools.summary: The dual ground of the conquest, the nations' sin and the promise to the fathers: 'not for thy righteousness, or for the uprightness of thine heart, dost thou go to possess their land: but for the wickedness of these nations the LORD thy God doth drive them out from before thee, and that he may perform the word which the LORD sware unto thy fathers, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — the conquest rested not on Israel's righteousness but on the nations' wickedness and God's oath to the patriarchs (the not for thy righteousness, or for the uprightness of thine heart, the merit again disclaimed, the but for the wickedness of these nations the LORD thy God doth drive them out, the nations' sin the ground of their judgment, the and that he may perform the word which the LORD sware unto thy fathers, the promise to the fathers the ground of Israel's blessing, the figure, the twofold ground, the nations' sin and God's faithful promise, neither of them Israel's merit, the truth that God's faithfulness to his covenant, not the people's worth, secured the inheritance, the figure of the covenant kept, the promise to the fathers fulfilled, the inheritance grounded on God's oath, Genesis 15:18, the grace that gives the land for the promise's sake, not the people's, the dual truth that humbles the people and exalts God's faithfulness); the merit disclaimed, the nations' sin the ground of judgment (the dual truth) — the disclaimer (not for righteousness); 'that he may perform the word which the LORD sware unto thy fathers' (the promise to the fathers the ground of the blessing, the covenant kept, Genesis 15:18, the figure, the inheritance grounded on God's oath, the providence, the dual ground, the people's stiffneckedness to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Not for thy righteousness, or for the uprightness of thine heart, dost thou go to possess their land: but for the wickedness of these nations the LORD thy God doth drive them out from before thee, and that he may perform the word which the LORD sware unto thy fathers, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
the dual ground of the conquest, the nations' sin and the promise to the fathers
the conquest grounded on the nations' wickedness and God's oath to the patriarchs, not Israel's righteousness
the covenant kept and the people humbled (Genesis 15:18)

schools.summary: The plain charge that Israel is a stiffnecked people, not righteous: 'understand therefore, that the LORD thy God giveth thee not this good land to possess it for thy righteousness; for thou art a stiffnecked people' — the stiffneckedness — 'the LORD thy God giveth thee not this good land to possess it for thy righteousness; for thou art a stiffnecked people': Moses drives home that the land is not given for Israel's righteousness, for they are a stubborn people (the witnesses noting the significance — the plain declaration of the people's sinfulness, the very opposite of righteousness, so far from deserving the land by their righteousness, they are a stiffnecked, stubborn people, prone to rebellion, the lesson — the humbling of all self-righteousness before God, the people told plainly they are not righteous but stiffnecked, that pride be cut off, the truth that God's people, far from earning his gifts, are sinners who provoke him, the inheritance all of grace, the truth that the heart of man is hard and stubborn, 'a stiffnecked people,' resistant to God, the proof against all merit, the figure — the doctrine of human sinfulness, the stiff neck that will not bend, 'ye stiffnecked... ye do always resist the Holy Ghost,' Acts 7:51, the humbling of the people that magnifies the grace of God, 'where is boasting then? It is excluded,' Romans 3:27, the truth that the recognition of one's own sinfulness is the ground of all true humility before grace, the figure of the stubborn heart that grace alone can save, the people's unworthiness the foil to God's free gift, the call to understand and own their stiffneckedness, that they not boast, the figure of the humbling of self-righteousness by the plain charge of the stubborn heart); the plain denial that the land is for their righteousness, the merit cut off (the inheritance of grace, Romans 3:27) — the denial (not for thy righteousness); 'for thou art a stiffnecked people' (the people plainly charged as stiffnecked and stubborn, the very opposite of righteous, the human sinfulness, the humbling of pride, Acts 7:51) — the figure (the stubborn heart that grace alone can save, the providence, the charge, the rebellions recalled to follow, vv.7-8) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does 'for thou art a stiffnecked people' establish?

sides.position: The plain declaration of the people's sinfulness, the very opposite of righteousness - the inheritance all of grace and merit cut off, a figure of the doctrine of human sinfulness, the stiff neck that resists God (Acts 7:51), the humbling that magnifies grace and excludes boasting (Romans 3:27)

themes: Understand therefore, that the LORD thy God giveth thee not this good land to possess it for thy righteousness; for thou art a stiffnecked people
the plain charge that Israel is a stiffnecked people, not righteous (the crux of human sinfulness and the humbling of self-righteousness)
the plain declaration that Israel is not righteous but stubborn, the inheritance all of grace and merit cut off
a figure of the doctrine of human sinfulness, the humbling that magnifies grace and excludes boasting (Acts 7:51; Romans 3:27)

schools.summary: The call to remember their constant provocation in the wilderness: 'remember, and forget not, how thou provokedst the LORD thy God to wrath in the wilderness: from the day that thou didst depart out of the land of Egypt, until ye came unto this place, ye have been rebellious against the LORD' — Moses calls the people to remember their continual provocation from Egypt to the present (the remember, and forget not, how thou provokedst the LORD thy God to wrath in the wilderness, the constant provocation, the from the day that thou didst depart out of the land of Egypt, until ye came unto this place, the whole journey, the ye have been rebellious against the LORD, the unbroken rebellion, the figure, the call to remember the long record of rebellion, the proof of their stiffneckedness, the truth that the people's whole history was one of provocation, the inheritance all of grace despite their sin, the figure of the remembrance of sin that humbles the heart, the long rebellion proving the people's unworthiness, the call to own their continual provocation, that grace be magnified, Psalm 106:7, the rebellion from Egypt to Moab the witness against all merit); the call to remember the provocation (the constant sin, Psalm 106:7) — the provocation (in the wilderness); 'ye have been rebellious against the LORD' (the rebellion from Egypt to the present, the whole history, Psalm 106:7, the figure, the long rebellion proving unworthiness, the providence, the rebellion, the golden calf recalled to follow, vv.8-21) — Henry, Gill.

themes: Remember, and forget not, how thou provokedst the LORD thy God to wrath in the wilderness: from the day that thou didst depart out of the land of Egypt, until ye came unto this place, ye have been rebellious against the LORD
the call to remember their constant provocation in the wilderness
the people's whole history one of provocation, the proof of their stiffneckedness and unworthiness
the inheritance all of grace despite their sin (Psalm 106:7)

schools.summary: The provocation at Horeb that nearly brought destruction: 'also in Horeb ye provoked the LORD to wrath, so that the LORD was angry with you to have destroyed you' — even at Horeb, the place of the law, the people provoked God by the golden calf, almost to their destruction (the also in Horeb ye provoked the LORD to wrath, the provocation at Sinai, the so that the LORD was angry with you to have destroyed you, God's anger to the point of destroying them, the figure, the provocation at the very mount of the law, the golden calf the height of their sin, the truth that even at Horeb, fresh from the giving of the law, they rebelled, the proof of their stiffneckedness, the figure of the sin that provokes God to the point of judgment, the calf at Sinai the great apostasy, Exodus 32:10, the wrath that would have destroyed them but for the mediator, the rebellion at the holiest place the deepest proof of their unworthiness); the provocation at Horeb by the golden calf (the sin at Sinai, Exodus 32:10) — the provocation (at Horeb); 'the LORD was angry with you to have destroyed you' (God's anger to the point of destruction, the judgment, Exodus 32:10, the figure, the sin that provokes God to judgment, the providence, the wrath, the forty days on the mount to follow, vv.9-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Also in Horeb ye provoked the LORD to wrath, so that the LORD was angry with you to have destroyed you
the provocation at Horeb that nearly brought destruction
the golden calf at the very mount of the law, the deepest proof of their stiffneckedness
the wrath that would have destroyed them but for the mediator (Exodus 32:10)

schools.summary: Moses on the mount forty days receiving the tables: 'when I was gone up into the mount to receive the tables of stone, even the tables of the covenant which the LORD made with you, then I abode in the mount forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water' — Moses recalls his forty days on the mount, fasting, to receive the tables of the covenant (the when I was gone up into the mount to receive the tables of stone, the ascent to receive the law, the even the tables of the covenant which the LORD made with you, the covenant tables, the then I abode in the mount forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water, the forty-day fast, the figure, Moses on the mount, fasting forty days, in close communion with God to receive the law, the truth that the mediator was with God on the people's behalf while they sinned below, the figure of the forty-day fast, the extraordinary nearness to God, 'he was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights,' Exodus 34:28, the contrast between Moses with God above and the people sinning below, the mediator receiving the covenant while the people broke it); Moses ascending to receive the covenant tables (the law given) — the ascent (to the mount); 'forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water' (the forty-day fast, the nearness to God, Exodus 34:28, the figure, the mediator with God while the people sinned, the providence, the fast, the giving of the tables to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When I was gone up into the mount to receive the tables of stone, even the tables of the covenant which the LORD made with you, then I abode in the mount forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water
Moses on the mount forty days receiving the tables
Moses fasting forty days in close communion with God to receive the covenant
the mediator with God above while the people sinned below (Exodus 34:28)

schools.summary: The two tables written with the finger of God: 'and the LORD delivered unto me two tables of stone written with the finger of God; and on them was written according to all the words, which the LORD spake with you in the mount out of the midst of the fire in the day of the assembly' — God gave Moses the two tables, written by his own finger, the very words spoken from the fire (the the LORD delivered unto me two tables of stone, the tables given, the written with the finger of God, the law written by God himself, the according to all the words, which the LORD spake with you in the mount out of the midst of the fire, the very words of God, the figure, the law written by the finger of God, the divine authorship of the ten commandments, the truth that the moral law is God's own writing, the abiding rule of his covenant, the figure of the law of God written by his finger, 'tables of stone, written with the finger of God,' Exodus 31:18, the words spoken from the fire and written on stone, the permanence and divine authority of the law, the covenant written by God, that the people might keep it); the tables written by God's finger (the divine law, Exodus 31:18) — the writing (by God's finger); 'according to all the words, which the LORD spake with you in the mount' (the very words of God written, the divine authority, Exodus 31:18, the figure, the law of God's own writing, the providence, the tables, the command to go down to the sinning people to follow, vv.11-12) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And the LORD delivered unto me two tables of stone written with the finger of God; and on them was written according to all the words, which the LORD spake with you in the mount out of the midst of the fire in the day of the assembly
the two tables written with the finger of God
God giving Moses the tables written by his own finger, the very words spoken from the fire
the moral law God's own writing and the abiding rule of his covenant (Exodus 31:18)

schools.summary: The giving of the covenant tables at the end of forty days: 'and it came to pass at the end of forty days and forty nights, that the LORD gave me the two tables of stone, even the tables of the covenant' — at the close of the forty days, God gave Moses the two tables of the covenant (the at the end of forty days and forty nights, the close of the fast, the the LORD gave me the two tables of stone, the tables given, the even the tables of the covenant, the covenant in writing, the figure, the covenant given in writing at the end of the forty days, the law committed to Moses, the truth that God gave the abiding rule of his covenant on stone, the moral law preserved, the figure of the covenant written and delivered, the law as the bond between God and his people, the tables of the covenant the witness of God's claim and the people's duty, the giving of the law that the people had already broken below, vv.12); the tables given at the end of the forty days (the covenant) — the giving (the tables); 'the tables of the covenant' (the covenant in writing, the bond, the figure, the law as the covenant bond, the providence, the tables, the command to go down to the corrupted people to follow, vv.12) — Gill.

themes: And it came to pass at the end of forty days and forty nights, that the LORD gave me the two tables of stone, even the tables of the covenant
the giving of the covenant tables at the end of forty days
God giving Moses the two tables of the covenant at the close of the forty days
the abiding rule of his covenant, the law that the people had already broken below

schools.summary: God's command to go down to the people who had corrupted themselves: 'and the LORD said unto me, Arise, get thee down quickly from hence; for thy people which thou hast brought forth out of Egypt have corrupted themselves; they are quickly turned aside out of the way which I commanded them; they have made them a molten image' — God bid Moses hurry down, for the people had quickly fallen into idolatry with the molten calf (the Arise, get thee down quickly, the urgent command, the thy people which thou hast brought forth out of Egypt have corrupted themselves, the people's corruption, the they are quickly turned aside out of the way which I commanded them; they have made them a molten image, the swift apostasy, the calf, the figure, the swiftness of their fall, turning aside so soon from the way, the truth that the people corrupted themselves quickly, the calf made even as the law was given, the figure of the proneness of the heart to idolatry, the swift turning from God, God calling them 'thy people' to Moses, as if disowning them in his anger, vv.13-14, the apostasy that proved their stiffneckedness, the molten image at the mount of the law, Exodus 32:7-8); the people corrupting themselves, the urgent command (the apostasy, Exodus 32:7) — the corruption (the people's fall); 'they are quickly turned aside... they have made them a molten image' (the swift apostasy with the molten calf, the idolatry, Exodus 32:8, the figure, the proneness to idolatry, the providence, the apostasy, God's anger to destroy them to follow, vv.13-14) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And the LORD said unto me, Arise, get thee down quickly from hence; for thy people which thou hast brought forth out of Egypt have corrupted themselves; they are quickly turned aside out of the way which I commanded them; they have made them a molten image
God's command to go down to the people who had corrupted themselves
the people falling so soon into idolatry with the molten calf even as the law was given
the proof of their stiffneckedness and proneness to sin (Exodus 32:7-8)

schools.summary: God declaring the people stiffnecked: 'furthermore the LORD spake unto me, saying, I have seen this people, and, behold, it is a stiffnecked people' — God himself declared the people stubborn and stiffnecked (the the LORD spake unto me, saying, I have seen this people, God's own judgment, the behold, it is a stiffnecked people, the divine verdict of their stubbornness, the figure, God himself owning the people as stiffnecked, the verdict from the searcher of hearts, the truth that the people's stubbornness was no exaggeration but God's own judgment, the figure of the divine verdict on the hard heart, the stiff neck that will not bend, the charge that justified the warning against self-righteousness, the people stiffnecked by God's own word, Exodus 32:9, the verdict that prepared the way for the threat of destruction, vv.14); God's own judgment of the people (the verdict, Exodus 32:9) — the verdict (God has seen); 'it is a stiffnecked people' (the divine verdict of stubbornness, the hard heart, Exodus 32:9, the figure, the verdict on the stubborn heart, the providence, the verdict, the threat of destruction to follow, vv.14) — Gill.

themes: Furthermore the LORD spake unto me, saying, I have seen this people, and, behold, it is a stiffnecked people
God declaring the people stiffnecked
God himself declaring the people stubborn, the verdict from the searcher of hearts
that justified the warning against all self-righteousness (Exodus 32:9)

schools.summary: God's purpose to destroy the people and make Moses a greater nation: 'let me alone, that I may destroy them, and blot out their name from under heaven: and I will make of thee a nation mightier and greater than they' — God threatened to destroy the people and make a new nation of Moses, yet bidding Moses 'let me alone' as if inviting his intercession (the let me alone, that I may destroy them, the threat of destruction, the and blot out their name from under heaven, the utter judgment, the and I will make of thee a nation mightier and greater than they, the offer to make Moses a great nation, the figure, the threat that tested and called forth the mediator's intercession, the truth that 'let me alone' gave Moses ground to plead, as if God's hands were held by the intercessor's prayer, the figure of the power of intercession, the mediator's prayer that stays the judgment, 'let me alone' the very door to his pleading, the offer to Moses refused for the people's sake, the mediator preferring the people's life to his own greatness, Exodus 32:10, the threat that magnified the mercy and the intercession); God's threat to destroy the people (the judgment, the door to intercession, Exodus 32:10) — the threat (let me alone); 'I will make of thee a nation mightier and greater than they' (the offer to make Moses a great nation, refused for the people's sake, the figure, the mediator preferring the people to his own greatness, the providence, the threat, Moses's intercession to follow, vv.18-19, 25-29) — JFB, Gill.

themes: Let me alone, that I may destroy them, and blot out their name from under heaven: and I will make of thee a nation mightier and greater than they

schools.summary: Moses coming down with the tables as the mount burned: 'so I turned and came down from the mount, and the mount burned with fire: and the two tables of the covenant were in my two hands' — Moses descended from the burning mount with the two tables of the covenant (the so I turned and came down from the mount, the descent, the and the mount burned with fire, the fire of God's presence, the and the two tables of the covenant were in my two hands, the tables borne down, the figure, Moses bearing the covenant down to the people who had broken it, the truth that the mediator came down with the law even as the people sinned against it, the figure of the descent with the covenant, the law brought to the corrupted people, the contrast between the holy law in Moses's hands and the calf in the camp, the tables of the covenant borne to a people unworthy of them, vv.16-17); Moses coming down from the burning mount (the descent) — the descent (from the mount); 'the two tables of the covenant were in my two hands' (the tables borne down, the covenant brought to the people, the figure, the law brought to the corrupted people, the providence, the descent, the sight of the calf to follow, vv.16) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: So I turned and came down from the mount, and the mount burned with fire: and the two tables of the covenant were in my two hands

schools.summary: Moses seeing the molten calf, the people turned aside: 'and I looked, and, behold, ye had sinned against the LORD your God, and had made you a molten calf: ye had turned aside quickly out of the way which the LORD had commanded you' — Moses saw the molten calf and the people's swift apostasy (the I looked, and, behold, ye had sinned against the LORD your God, the sin seen, the and had made you a molten calf, the idol, the ye had turned aside quickly out of the way which the LORD had commanded you, the swift apostasy, the figure, the sight of the calf, the people's sin laid bare before the mediator's eyes, the truth that the people had sinned grievously and swiftly, the calf the proof, the figure of the apostasy seen and judged, the swift turning from God, the contrast between the law just given and the idol already made, Exodus 32:19, the sin that broke the covenant even as it was being given); Moses seeing the sin and the calf (the apostasy, Exodus 32:19) — the sight (the calf seen); 'ye had turned aside quickly out of the way' (the swift turning from God, the apostasy, Exodus 32:19, the figure, the apostasy seen and judged, the providence, the sight, the breaking of the tables to follow, vv.17) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And I looked, and, behold, ye had sinned against the LORD your God, and had made you a molten calf: ye had turned aside quickly out of the way which the LORD had commanded you

schools.summary: Moses breaking the tables before the people's eyes: 'and I took the two tables, and cast them out of my two hands, and brake them before your eyes' — Moses cast down and broke the tables of the covenant before the people (the I took the two tables, and cast them out of my two hands, the breaking, the and brake them before your eyes, the public breaking, the figure, the breaking of the tables a sign that the covenant was broken by the people's sin, the truth that the people had already broken the covenant, the broken tables the visible token, the figure of the covenant broken by sin, the law's tables shattered as the people had shattered the bond, the breaking before their eyes a solemn witness against them, the broken tables the sign that the people had forfeited the covenant by the calf, Exodus 32:19, the law broken, that the mercy might be the more magnified in its renewal); Moses casting down the tables (the breaking, Exodus 32:19) — the breaking (the tables cast down); 'brake them before your eyes' (the public breaking, the covenant broken by sin, the token, Exodus 32:19, the figure, the broken covenant, the providence, the breaking, Moses's intercession to follow, vv.18-19) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And I took the two tables, and cast them out of my two hands, and brake them before your eyes

schools.summary: Moses's second forty days of fasting and intercession for the people's sin: 'and I fell down before the LORD, as at the first, forty days and forty nights: I did neither eat bread, nor drink water, because of all your sins which ye had sinned, in doing wickedly in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger' — the mediator's intercession — 'I fell down before the LORD, as at the first, forty days and forty nights: I did neither eat bread, nor drink water, because of all your sins which ye had sinned': Moses fell before God a second forty days, fasting, interceding for the people's sin (the witnesses noting the significance — the mediator's intercession for a sinful people, prostrate and fasting forty days for their sake, Moses, the mediator, taking the people's sin upon his heart, pleading for them with extraordinary fasting and prayer, the lesson — the power and labour of intercession, the mediator standing between the sinful people and God's wrath, the second forty-day fast, 'because of all your sins,' the burden of the people's guilt borne in prayer, the truth that the mediator's prayer turns away the wrath, 'had not Moses his chosen stood before him in the breach, to turn away his wrath,' Psalm 106:23, the figure — Moses a type of Christ the true Mediator, who ever lives to make intercession for his people, 'he is able... to save them to the uttermost... seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them,' Hebrews 7:25, the figure of the intercessor who pleads for sinners and turns away the judgment, the forty-day fast a figure of the costly intercession of the Mediator, the truth that God's people are saved through the prayer of the mediator, fulfilled in Christ who bore our sins and intercedes, the figure of the mediator's labour and self-denial for the people's salvation, the figure of the mediator's intercession that turns away wrath, fulfilled in Christ); Moses prostrate and fasting a second forty days, the labour of intercession (the mediator's prayer, Psalm 106:23) — the fast (the intercession); Moses bearing the burden of the people's sin in prayer, the mediator standing in the breach (the burden of guilt, the intercession that turns away wrath, Hebrews 7:25) — the figure (Moses a type of Christ the Mediator who intercedes, the providence, the intercession, the fear of God's anger to follow, vv.19) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Moses's forty-day fasting intercession reveal?

sides.position: The power and labour of the mediator's intercession - Moses prostrate and fasting for the people's sin, standing in the breach to turn away God's wrath by prayer (Psalm 106:23), a figure of Christ the true Mediator who bore our sins and ever lives to make intercession for his people (Hebrews 7:25)

themes: And I fell down before the LORD, as at the first, forty days and forty nights: I did neither eat bread, nor drink water, because of all your sins which ye had sinned, in doing wickedly in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger

schools.summary: Moses's fear of God's anger and God hearing his prayer: 'for I was afraid of the anger and hot displeasure, wherewith the LORD was wroth against you to destroy you. But the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also' — Moses feared God's wrath that would have destroyed the people, but God heard his intercession (the I was afraid of the anger and hot displeasure, the fear of God's wrath, the wherewith the LORD was wroth against you to destroy you, the threatened destruction, the But the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also, the prayer answered, the figure, the mediator's fear of the wrath, and the mercy that heard his prayer, the truth that the intercession turned away the wrath, God hearing the mediator, the figure of the prayer that prevails, the wrath stayed by the intercessor's plea, 'the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much,' James 5:16, the mercy that heard Moses and spared the people, the intercession prevailing, the God who hears the mediator and turns away the judgment, fulfilled in Christ heard for his people); Moses's fear of the wrath that would destroy them (the threatened judgment) — the fear (of God's anger); 'the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also' (God hearing the intercession, the prayer prevailing, James 5:16, the figure, the prayer that turns away wrath, the providence, the answer, the prayer for Aaron to follow, vv.20) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: For I was afraid of the anger and hot displeasure, wherewith the LORD was wroth against you to destroy you. But the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also

the prayer of the mediator that turns away the judgment (James 5:16)

schools.summary: Moses's prayer for Aaron, who had made the calf: 'and the LORD was very angry with Aaron to have destroyed him: and I prayed for Aaron also the same time' — God was angry enough to destroy Aaron for making the calf, but Moses prayed for him (the the LORD was very angry with Aaron to have destroyed him, Aaron's guilt and danger, the and I prayed for Aaron also the same time, Moses's intercession for Aaron, the figure, the mediator interceding even for the guilty Aaron, who had led the people into the calf, the truth that Aaron, though high priest, was guilty and in danger, spared only by intercession, the figure of the intercession that reaches even the chief offender, Moses praying for his own brother, the mercy that spared Aaron at the mediator's prayer, the guilty saved by intercession, the truth that even the leaders need the mediator's prayer, none righteous in themselves, vv.18); God's anger against Aaron for the calf (the guilt of the high priest) — the guilt (Aaron in danger); 'I prayed for Aaron also the same time' (Moses's prayer for the guilty Aaron, the intercession for the chief offender, the figure, the mediator's prayer reaching even the guilty, the providence, the prayer, the burning of the calf to follow, vv.21) — JFB, Gill.

schools.summary: Moses destroying the calf, grinding it to dust: 'and I took your sin, the calf which ye had made, and burnt it with fire, and stamped it, and ground it very small, even until it was as small as dust: and I cast the dust thereof into the brook that descended out of the mount' — Moses utterly destroyed the calf, burning and grinding it to dust and casting it into the brook (the I took your sin, the calf which ye had made, the calf called their sin, the and burnt it with fire, and stamped it, and ground it very small, the utter destruction, the I cast the dust thereof into the brook that descended out of the mount, the dust cast away, the figure, the total destruction of the idol, the sin utterly removed, the truth that the idol was reduced to nothing, the calf called 'your sin,' the object of their sin destroyed, the figure of the thoroughness with which sin and its idols must be destroyed, ground to dust and cast away, the idol made nothing, that they see the vanity of what they worshipped, Exodus 32:20, the destruction that left no trace of the idol, the sin purged); the calf utterly destroyed, ground to dust (the idol made nothing, Exodus 32:20) — the destruction (the calf ground small); 'I cast the dust thereof into the brook' (the dust cast away, the idol gone, Exodus 32:20, the figure, the sin and idol utterly removed, the providence, the destruction, the other rebellions recalled to follow, vv.22-24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The provocations at Taberah, Massah, and Kibroth-hattaavah: 'and at Taberah, and at Massah, and at Kibrothhattaavah, ye provoked the LORD to wrath' — Moses recalls other places where the people provoked God (the at Taberah, the place of the fire, the murmuring, Numbers 11:1-3, the and at Massah, the place of the tempting, the strife for water, Exodus 17:7, the and at Kibrothhattaavah, the graves of lust, the craving for flesh, Numbers 11:34, the ye provoked the LORD to wrath, the repeated provocation, the figure, the catalogue of rebellions, the people provoking God at place after place, the truth that the golden calf was no isolated sin but part of a long record of provocation, the figure of the unbroken rebellion, the people murmuring and lusting and tempting God repeatedly, the proof of their stiffneckedness in the many places of provocation, the rebellions named that humble the people and magnify the grace, Psalm 78:40); the provocations at Taberah and Massah (the rebellions, Numbers 11:3; Exodus 17:7) — the provocations (at the places); 'at Kibrothhattaavah, ye provoked the LORD to wrath' (the lust for flesh, the provocation, Numbers 11:34, the figure, the unbroken rebellion, the providence, the rebellions, the unbelief at Kadesh to follow, vv.23) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The rebellion at Kadesh, refusing to go up in unbelief: 'likewise when the LORD sent you from Kadeshbarnea, saying, Go up and possess the land which I have given you; then ye rebelled against the commandment of the LORD your God, and ye believed him not, nor hearkened to his voice' — at Kadesh the people refused to go up, rebelling and not believing God (the when the LORD sent you from Kadeshbarnea, saying, Go up and possess the land, the command to go up, the then ye rebelled against the commandment of the LORD your God, the rebellion, the and ye believed him not, nor hearkened to his voice, the unbelief at the root, the figure, the rebellion at Kadesh, the unbelief that refused the land, the truth that the root of their rebellion was unbelief, the refusal to trust God's word, the figure of the unbelief that forfeited the rest, 'they could not enter in because of unbelief,' Hebrews 3:19, the great rebellion at Kadesh, the worst of all, that brought the forty years' wandering, the unbelief that is the heart of all rebellion, the people not believing nor obeying); the rebellion at Kadesh, refusing to go up (the disobedience) — the rebellion (at Kadesh); 'ye believed him not, nor hearkened to his voice' (the unbelief at the root, the heart of the rebellion, Hebrews 3:19, the figure, the unbelief that forfeited the rest, the providence, the rebellion, the summary of their rebelliousness to follow, vv.24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

the people refusing to go up at Kadesh, the unbelief at the root of their rebellion

schools.summary: The summary, that Israel had been rebellious from the first: 'ye have been rebellious against the LORD from the day that I knew you' — Moses sums up the whole record: the people had been rebellious from the beginning (the ye have been rebellious against the LORD, the unbroken rebellion, the from the day that I knew you, from the very first, the whole time, the figure, the summary of the people's whole history as rebellion, the truth that from the day Moses knew them they had been rebellious, the proof of their stiffneckedness complete, the figure of the long and unbroken rebellion, the people never faithful but always provoking, the verdict that humbles all pride and magnifies the grace that bore with them, the rebellion from first to last the witness against all merit, Isaiah 48:8); the people's unbroken rebellion (the verdict, Isaiah 48:8) — the rebellion (against the LORD); 'from the day that I knew you' (from the very first, the unbroken record, Isaiah 48:8, the figure, the long rebellion proving their nature, the providence, the summary, Moses's intercession recounted to follow, vv.25-29) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: Moses recounting his forty days of prostration for the people: 'thus I fell down before the LORD forty days and forty nights, as I fell down at the first; because the LORD had said he would destroy you' — Moses recounts again his forty days of prostration in prayer, because God had threatened to destroy them (the I fell down before the LORD forty days and forty nights, the prostration in prayer, the as I fell down at the first, the second forty days, as the first, the because the LORD had said he would destroy you, the ground, the threatened destruction, the figure, the mediator prostrate forty days, pleading against the threatened destruction, the truth that the intercessor lay before God for the people's life, the prayer that stood between them and judgment, the figure of the persevering intercession, the mediator prostrate for the people's sake, the prayer that prevailed against the wrath, the mediator's labour for the guilty, the intercession recounted to set forth the mercy and the prayer, Psalm 106:23); Moses prostrate forty days in prayer (the intercession, Psalm 106:23) — the prostration (forty days); 'because the LORD had said he would destroy you' (the threatened destruction the ground of the prayer, the intercession against wrath, the figure, the mediator pleading for the people's life, the providence, the intercession, the plea itself to follow, vv.26-29) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Thus I fell down before the LORD forty days and forty nights, as I fell down at the first; because the LORD had said he would destroy you

the mediator prostrate forty days, pleading against the threatened destruction

schools.summary: Moses's plea, 'destroy not thy people whom thou hast redeemed': 'I prayed therefore unto the LORD, and said, O Lord GOD, destroy not thy people and thine inheritance, which thou hast redeemed through thy greatness, which thou hast brought forth out of Egypt with a mighty hand' — Moses pleaded that God not destroy the people he had redeemed (the I prayed therefore unto the LORD, the prayer, the O Lord GOD, destroy not thy people and thine inheritance, the plea for the people, the which thou hast redeemed through thy greatness... out of Egypt with a mighty hand, the appeal to the redemption, the figure, the mediator's plea grounded on God's own ownership and redemption of the people, the truth that the intercessor pleads not the people's worth but God's relation to them, 'thy people, thine inheritance, whom thou hast redeemed,' the figure of the prayer that appeals to God's own glory and work, the redeemed not to be destroyed, the plea that rests on God's investment in his people, 'thou hast redeemed,' the ground of the appeal, Exodus 32:11, the intercession that pleads God's own honour in his people); Moses pleading for the people as God's own (the appeal, Exodus 32:11) — the plea (destroy not thy people); 'which thou hast redeemed through thy greatness... out of Egypt' (the appeal to the redemption, God's own work, Exodus 32:11, the figure, the prayer grounded on God's relation to his people, the providence, the plea, the appeal to the fathers and God's name to follow, vv.27-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: I prayed therefore unto the LORD, and said, O Lord GOD, destroy not thy people and thine inheritance, which thou hast redeemed through thy greatness, which thou hast brought forth out of Egypt with a mighty hand

the mediator pleading not the people's worth but God's relation to them, his people, his inheritance, whom he had redeemed
the prayer that appeals to God's own glory and work (Exodus 32:11)

schools.summary: Moses's plea to remember the fathers and not the people's stubbornness: 'remember thy servants, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; look not unto the stubbornness of this people, nor to their wickedness, nor to their sin' — the appeal to the covenant — 'Remember thy servants, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; look not unto the stubbornness of this people, nor to their wickedness, nor to their sin': Moses pleaded God's covenant with the fathers and asked him not to regard the people's sin (the witnesses noting the significance — the intercession grounded on the covenant with the patriarchs, not on the people's worth, the plea to 'remember the fathers,' the covenant and promise, while asking God to 'look not' upon the people's stubbornness and sin, the lesson — that the ground of acceptance is God's covenant, not human merit, the mediator pleads the promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and turns God's eye away from the people's guilt to his own faithfulness, the intercession that rests wholly on grace and covenant, not on the sinner's deserving, the truth that God's mercy flows from his covenant, 'he hath remembered his covenant for ever,' Psalm 105:8, 'for thy servant David's sake,' 1 Kings 11:13, the figure — the intercession of Christ who pleads not our worth but the covenant of grace, the merits of the true Seed, the figure of the prayer that asks God to look away from the sinner's guilt to the covenant, 'look not unto the stubbornness of this people,' the truth that all acceptance is for the sake of the covenant and the promised Seed, not the sinner's righteousness, the figure of the mediator pleading the covenant and the fathers, that God's faithfulness, not the people's sin, decide their fate, the figure of intercession grounded on the covenant, the plea of the fathers against the people's sin); Moses pleading the covenant and promise to the patriarchs, the ground of the appeal (the covenant remembered, Psalm 105:8) — the plea (remember the fathers); Moses asking God to look not on the people's sin but on the covenant, the acceptance grounded on grace not merit (the plea against the people's guilt, 1 Kings 11:13) — the figure (Christ's intercession that pleads the covenant, not our worth, the providence, the plea, the appeal to God's name before the nations to follow, vv.28) — Calvin, Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Moses's plea 'remember the fathers, look not unto the stubbornness of this people' establish about intercession?

sides.position: That the ground of acceptance is God's covenant, not human merit - the mediator pleads the promise to the fathers and turns God's eye from the people's sin to his own faithfulness (Psalm 105:8), a figure of the intercession of Christ who pleads not our worth but the covenant of grace and the merits of the true Seed (1 Kings 11:13)

themes: Remember thy servants, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; look not unto the stubbornness of this people, nor to their wickedness, nor to their sin

the plea that rests on God's covenant with the fathers, not on the people's worth, asking him to look away from their sin to his own faithfulness
a figure of the intercession of Christ who pleads not our merit but the covenant of grace (Psalm 105:8; 1 Kings 11:13)

schools.summary: Moses pleading God's honour before the nations: 'lest the land whence thou broughtest us out say, Because the LORD was not able to bring them into the land which he promised them, and because he hated them, he hath brought them out to slay them in the wilderness' — Moses pleaded that destroying the people would let Egypt blaspheme, as if God could not or would not bring them in (the lest the land whence thou broughtest us out say, the danger of Egypt's blasphemy, the Because the LORD was not able to bring them into the land which he promised them, the slander of God's power, the and because he hated them, he hath brought them out to slay them in the wilderness, the slander of God's love, the figure, the mediator pleading God's own honour, lest the nations blaspheme his power and love, the truth that the intercessor appeals to God's glory among the heathen, that his name be not dishonoured, the figure of the prayer that pleads God's reputation, 'what wilt thou do unto thy great name?' Joshua 7:9, the appeal to God's honour the height of intercession, that his power and faithfulness be vindicated, the plea that God spare the people for his own name's sake, Numbers 14:16); the danger of Egypt's blasphemy of God's power (the appeal, Numbers 14:16) — the slander (God not able); 'because he hated them, he hath brought them out to slay them' (the slander of God's love, the blasphemy, Joshua 7:9, the figure, the appeal to God's honour, the providence, the appeal, the closing plea of the inheritance to follow, vv.29) — Gill.

schools.summary: The closing plea, 'they are thy people and thine inheritance': 'yet they are thy people and thine inheritance, which thou broughtest out by thy mighty power and by thy stretched out arm' — Moses closes his plea by owning the people as God's own, redeemed by his power (the yet they are thy people and thine inheritance, the appeal to God's ownership, the which thou broughtest out by thy mighty power and by thy stretched out arm, the redemption by God's power, the figure, the mediator's closing plea, resting all on God's ownership and redemption of the people, the truth that the people, sinful as they were, belonged to God, his inheritance, redeemed by his mighty arm, the figure of the intercession that pleads God's own claim, 'thine they were, and thou gavest them me,' John 17:6, the people God's possession, not to be destroyed, the redemption the ground of the plea, the appeal that prevailed, the people spared as God's own inheritance, Psalm 33:12); the people owned as God's own (the appeal, Psalm 33:12) — the ownership (thy people); 'which thou broughtest out by thy mighty power and by thy stretched out arm' (the redemption by God's power, the ground of the plea, John 17:6, the figure, the people God's possession by redemption, the providence, the closing plea, the renewal of the covenant to follow, ch.10) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Yet they are thy people and thine inheritance, which thou broughtest out by thy mighty power and by thy stretched out arm
